new_options.rationale: Home self-initiated prednisolone is reserved for selected children with frequent severe exacerbations under specialist guidance and a tailored plan; not routine yellow-zone advice in primary care.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Stop the preventer and rely on salbutamol alone until the worsening symptoms resolve
new_options.rationale: Stopping the preventer during a deterioration worsens control; preventer should be continued or temporarily intensified.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Salbutamol 4-6 puffs via spacer every 4 hours; temporarily increase preventer (e.g. quadruple ICS or as specified in her plan); GP review within 24-48 hours; clear red-flag escalation criteria (no relief from salbutamol, severe breathlessness, blue lips, inability to speak in sentences) prompting call-000 or ED
new_options.rationale: The National Asthma Council yellow zone increases salbutamol to 4-6 puffs via spacer every 4 hours, continues or temporarily steps up preventer, and arranges GP review within 24-48 hours with clear red-flag criteria for ED. Written zone-based plans reduce hospitalisation and time off school.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

new_options.text: Present immediately to ED for any increase in salbutamol use regardless of severity
new_options.rationale: Routine ED presentation for any salbutamol increase produces unnecessary attendances and undermines self-management; clear thresholds for home vs GP vs ED are the purpose of the action plan.
source_refs: National Asthma Council Australian Asthma Handbook

verdicts.new_stem: A 14-month-old boy (weight 11 kg) presents to a regional ED with 12 hours of barking cough, hoarse cry and worsening stridor at rest. He received nebulised adrenaline 5 mg (1:1000) and oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg 45 minutes ago. He had transient improvement for 20 minutes but is now again stridulous at rest with worsening recessions, increasing work of breathing, intermittent quiet episodes followed by paradoxical chest movement. He is restless and difficult to console, and SpO2 is dropping. There is no drooling, no fever above 38.0 deg C and no foreign body history. He is fully immunised. The anaesthetic and ENT registrars have been paged and are 5 minutes away. The team is preparing the difficult-airway trolley. Current observations: HR 168, RR 48, SpO2 88 percent on 8 L/min Hudson mask, BP 102/66, T 37.6 deg C, with marked biphasic stridor, severe recessions, intermittent paradoxical chest wall movement and circumoral cyanosis.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Wait 30 minutes for dexamethasone to take full effect before any further intervention
new_options.rationale: He has impending respiratory failure (hypoxia, exhaustion, paradoxical breathing); passive waiting is unsafe.

new_options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline 5 mg and continue ward observation; defer airway team involvement
new_options.rationale: Repeat adrenaline is reasonable as a bridge but does not substitute for definitive airway when impending respiratory failure is present.

new_options.text: Commence high-flow nasal cannula oxygen 2 L/kg/min and reassess at 30 minutes without airway team involvement
new_options.rationale: HFNC does not bypass critical subglottic narrowing and must not delay airway team activation when impending failure is present.

new_options.text: Repeat nebulised adrenaline as a bridge while transferring with the airway team to theatre for gas inhalational induction by anaesthetics, with ENT present and a smaller-than-predicted ETT prepared
new_options.rationale: Severe croup unresponsive to initial adrenaline plus steroid with signs of impending respiratory failure requires definitive airway management in theatre under inhalational induction (preserving spontaneous ventilation), with ENT standby for surgical airway and a smaller-than-predicted ETT. Repeat adrenaline acts as a bridge during transfer.

new_options.text: Rapid sequence intubation in the ED with rocuronium 1 mg/kg and an age-appropriate cuffed ETT
new_options.rationale: RSI with paralysis risks losing the airway when bag-mask ventilation may be difficult. Inhalational induction preserving spontaneous ventilation in theatre is the standard approach (the RSI-in-ED anti-pattern tested here).

verdicts.new_stem: A 4-year-old unimmunised boy presents to a regional ED with 6 hours of high fever to 39.4 deg C, painful drooling, refusal to swallow and a muffled hot-potato voice. He sits forward on his mother's lap in a tripod posture with his neck extended. There has been no preceding barking cough and no hoarseness; the parents describe rapid onset over a single afternoon. There is no history of choking on a small object, no recent dental work, and no preceding pharyngitis. He has not received nebulised adrenaline or steroid. The ED consultant has asked the team to avoid distressing the child and to call anaesthetics and ENT immediately. He is alert but anxious and looks toxic. HR 148, RR 32, SpO2 95 percent on room air, BP 102/64, T 39.4 deg C, with tripoding, drooling, mild inspiratory stridor and no barking cough.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and immediate next step (without distressing examination) is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Acute epiglottitis (most likely Haemophilus influenzae type b given non-immunisation); keep the child calm on the parent's lap and proceed to theatre with anaesthetics and ENT for gas inhalational induction and definitive airway before any blood tests or imaging
new_options.rationale: Rapid onset of fever, drooling, muffled voice and tripoding without barking cough in an unimmunised child is classic for Hib epiglottitis. Definitive airway management in theatre by anaesthetics with ENT standby precedes investigations; cannulation, bloods and imaging are deferred until the airway is secured because distress can precipitate complete obstruction, per RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Viral laryngotracheobronchitis (croup); give oral dexamethasone and nebulised adrenaline in ED
new_options.rationale: Croup has a coryzal prodrome with barking cough and hoarseness, not drooling, muffled voice or toxic appearance.

new_options.text: Inhaled foreign body; arrange urgent rigid bronchoscopy in theatre
new_options.rationale: FB aspiration follows a witnessed choking event with sudden cough and focal asymmetry; absence of choking history and presence of high fever and toxic appearance make this unlikely.

new_options.text: Bacterial tracheitis complicating viral croup with Staphylococcus aureus; intubate in theatre with copious tracheal toilet
new_options.rationale: Bacterial tracheitis usually follows several days of croup-like illness; the rapid single-afternoon onset and tripoding favour epiglottitis.

new_options.text: Retropharyngeal abscess; arrange contrast neck CT before airway management
new_options.rationale: Retropharyngeal abscess usually presents with neck stiffness or torticollis and a preceding URTI or dental focus; CT-before-airway in a tripoding child with impending obstruction is unsafe.

verdicts.new_stem: A 6-day-old male term infant (birth weight 3.4 kg, now 3.2 kg) is brought to a regional ED with 12 hours of poor feeding, lethargy and grey colour. He was born at 39+4 weeks with Apgars 9 and 9 and discharged at 36 hours after a normal newborn examination. The newborn pulse oximetry screen at 24 hours was normal. There is no family history of congenital heart disease. He is grey-mottled with cool peripheries and weak femoral pulses bilaterally. Four-limb BPs: right arm 78/50, right leg 52/30. Pre-ductal SpO2 (right hand) 92 percent, post-ductal SpO2 (foot) 78 percent. The precordium is hyperdynamic with a single loud S2 and no audible murmur. VBG: pH 7.18, lactate 6.2, BE -12, glucose 3.1, Na 136, K 4.6, Hb 168. Chest x-ray shows cardiomegaly with pulmonary plethora. Echocardiography reveals critical coarctation with a closing PDA.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention and oxygen target?

new_options.text: IV indomethacin 0.2 mg/kg to close the PDA
new_options.rationale: Closing the duct in a duct-dependent systemic circulation precipitates cardiovascular collapse; the goal is to keep the duct OPEN.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

new_options.text: Adenosine 0.1 mg/kg rapid IV push for suspected supraventricular tachycardia
new_options.rationale: There is no narrow-complex tachycardia over 220 bpm with absent P waves; the picture is duct-dependent systemic shock, not SVT.

new_options.text: Oral sildenafil 0.5 mg/kg to reduce pulmonary vascular resistance
new_options.rationale: Sildenafil is used in persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn, not duct-dependent systemic lesions; it does not maintain ductal patency.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

new_options.text: IV prostaglandin E1 infusion starting at 10-50 ng/kg/min (about 0.01-0.05 microgram/kg/min) titrated to effect, with airway support readiness for apnoea; target pre-ductal SpO2 about 85-92 percent and avoid high FiO2
new_options.rationale: Duct-dependent systemic circulation with a closing PDA is reopened with prostaglandin E1 at 0.01-0.05 microgram/kg/min titrated to effect, with airway readiness for PGE-induced apnoea. High FiO2 is avoided because oxygen promotes ductal closure; target pre-ductal SpO2 about 85-92 percent.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

new_options.text: Commence head-box oxygen at FiO2 1.0 to maximise systemic saturations
new_options.rationale: High FiO2 promotes ductal closure and worsens systemic perfusion; this is the oxygen-trap anti-pattern in duct-dependent systemic lesions.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Duct dependent congenital heart disease

verdicts.new_stem: A 4-month-old girl is brought to a winter morning ED with a 3-day history of clear rhinorrhoea and a prominent wet cough. Her parents report she has been working harder to breathe today and is taking about 50 percent of her usual formula. She was born at term with no neonatal complications. She is alert and interactive, with moderate intercostal and subcostal recessions, widespread fine inspiratory crackles and a high-pitched expiratory wheeze bilaterally. HR 150, RR 65, T 37.8 deg C, SpO2 94 percent on room air. There has been no apnoea witnessed. She is fully immunised for age and has no congenital heart disease. She has not received any salbutamol or steroid.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial management plan?

new_options.text: Nebulised salbutamol trial 2.5 mg, reassess at 20 minutes; if responsive continue every 4 hours
new_options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended in routine bronchiolitis under 12 months; the pathology is mucus plugging and mucosal oedema rather than smooth-muscle bronchospasm.

new_options.text: Single oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg with discharge home if SpO2 stays over 92 percent
new_options.rationale: Steroids do not change outcomes in bronchiolitis; this is not croup or asthma.

new_options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone for possible bacterial coinfection
new_options.rationale: Antibiotics are not indicated without focal bacterial features (no consolidation, no toxic appearance).

new_options.text: Discharge home on small frequent oral feeds with safety-net advice; return if feeding under 50 percent or any apnoea
new_options.rationale: She already meets the AU admission threshold of feeding 50 percent of usual; discharging is unsafe under RCH CPG criteria.

new_options.text: Admit for supportive care: minimal handling, nasogastric or IV hydration because feeding is 50 percent of usual; supplemental oxygen if SpO2 drops below 92 percent; close monitoring for apnoea
new_options.rationale: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis criteria for admission include feeding under 50 percent, persistent SpO2 below 92 percent on room air, apnoea or severe work of breathing. This infant meets the feeding threshold and has moderate work of breathing; supportive care (hydration, oxygen only if SpO2 below 92 percent) is the mainstay.

verdicts.new_stem: A 6-week-old term infant presents to ED in mid-winter with 3 days of coryza, cough and increasing work of breathing. He has been feeding half his usual volume for 24 hours and has had 2 wet nappies today. He is fully immunised for age, has no congenital heart disease and takes no regular medicines. His older sibling has a runny nose and he attends daycare. He is alert but tired with subcostal recessions, fine widespread crackles and wheeze without focal signs. T 37.8 deg C, HR 168, RR 62, SpO2 94 percent on room air; capillary refill 2 seconds. There is no apnoea witnessed.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Start oral amoxicillin and arrange GP review tomorrow
new_options.rationale: No focal bacterial features and antibiotics do not change outcomes in typical bronchiolitis.

new_options.text: Admit for supportive care: suction PRN, NG or IV hydration because feeding is 50 percent of usual and wet nappies are reduced, supplemental oxygen only if SpO2 falls below 92 percent, observation for apnoea given age under 8 weeks
new_options.rationale: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis admits if feeding under 50 percent, SpO2 persistently below 92 percent, apnoea or severe work of breathing. This 6-week-old meets the feeding threshold and is in the under-8-week apnoea risk window, justifying admission. Oxygen titration to SpO2 over 92 percent rather than 94 percent is the Australian threshold.

new_options.text: Order a chest x-ray and start IV ceftriaxone
new_options.rationale: Imaging and broad antibiotics are not routine in typical bronchiolitis.

new_options.text: Give nebulised salbutamol and reassess at 20 minutes
new_options.rationale: Bronchodilators are not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months.

new_options.text: Administer dexamethasone and nebulised adrenaline
new_options.rationale: Adrenaline plus steroid is the croup pathway, not bronchiolitis (no barking cough or stridor).

verdicts.new_stem: A 10-month-old boy presents to ED with 8 hours of intermittent screaming episodes, drawing up of the legs and repeated non-bilious vomiting. Between episodes he is pale and settles briefly but has become increasingly lethargic. His mother reports a mild viral illness last week. He was born at term, has had no previous surgery and is fully immunised. He is afebrile, mildly dehydrated, with a soft but mildly distended abdomen. A small amount of red mucus is seen in the nappy after a pain episode. There is no bilious vomiting and no peritonism. HR 156, RR 30, BP 86/54, SpO2 99 percent on room air.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step and how should management proceed if the imaging is positive?

new_options.text: Abdominal ultrasound now; if a target sign with a doughnut or pseudokidney appearance is seen and there is no peritonitis or perforation, proceed to air-contrast enema reduction by interventional radiology after surgical consultation and IV resuscitation
new_options.rationale: Episodic colicky pain, lethargy, vomiting and red-mucus stool are classic intussusception. Ultrasound is first-line (target/doughnut sign). Once confirmed without contraindications (peritonitis, perforation, shock not responsive to resuscitation), air-contrast (or saline) enema reduction by radiology with surgical standby is the AU first-line definitive management; surgical reduction is reserved for failed enema or contraindications.

new_options.text: Plain abdominal x-ray and treat empirically as gastroenteritis
new_options.rationale: AXR can show obstruction or a soft-tissue mass but is not the best first test when intussusception is suspected; empirical gastroenteritis treatment misses a surgical emergency.

new_options.text: Urgent upper GI contrast study to exclude malrotation with volvulus
new_options.rationale: Upper GI contrast is for bilious vomiting concerning for malrotation; this presentation is colicky pain with red-mucus stool pointing to intussusception.

new_options.text: CT abdomen with IV contrast as initial imaging
new_options.rationale: CT is not first-line in children with suspected intussusception because of radiation; ultrasound is preferred.

new_options.text: Stool PCR for infectious gastroenteritis and observe in short stay
new_options.rationale: Episodic colicky pain with red mucus is not gastroenteritis; stool testing delays diagnosis of a surgical emergency.

verdicts.new_stem: An 18-day-old male infant is brought to ED with 6 hours of feeling hot to touch and feeding less vigorously. He was born at 39+3 weeks NVD with maternal GBS screen negative at 36 weeks; membranes ruptured 4 hours pre-delivery. He has been well since birth and is exclusively breastfed. He is quiet but responsive with a weak cry. Axillary temperature is 38.1 deg C (rectal 38.3). HR 175, RR 48, SpO2 97 percent on room air, central CRT 3 seconds with cool peripheries. Anterior fontanelle is flat. He has mild subcostal recession with clear breath sounds and no rash. Birth weight 3.4 kg, current 3.6 kg. His 2-year-old sibling had a URTI last week.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management plan?

new_options.text: Perform full septic workup (FBC, CRP, blood culture, catheter urine, lumbar puncture) and commence IV benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus IV gentamicin plus IV cefotaxime; admit
new_options.rationale: Australian neonatal practice (RCH CPG: Febrile child, Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic) for a febrile infant 7 days to under 4 weeks: full septic workup including LP, and empirical IV benzylpenicillin/ampicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime (Listeria, Gram-negatives, CNS penetration). Note: ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates (bilirubin displacement) so cefotaxime is preferred at this age. All febrile neonates are admitted.

new_options.text: Partial septic workup without lumbar puncture because the infant looks relatively well; commence IV cefotaxime monotherapy and admit
new_options.rationale: Clinical appearance does not exclude meningitis in this age group; LP is part of the mandatory workup. Cefotaxime alone misses Listeria.

new_options.text: Observe 4 hours in short stay; discharge with GP follow-up at 24 hours if afebrile and feeding improved
new_options.rationale: SBI risk in febrile neonates is 10-15 percent; observation and discharge is unsafe.

new_options.text: Blood and urine cultures plus a single dose IM ceftriaxone, admit for 48 hours observation, perform LP only on deterioration
new_options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates; IM single-dose therapy is inadequate; LP cannot be deferred.

new_options.text: Full septic workup with IV ceftriaxone plus IV aciclovir; admit
new_options.rationale: Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates and is not first-line (ampicillin/benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime is the AU regimen for 7-day to under 4-week infants). Aciclovir is added only when HSV is clinically suspected (maternal vesicles, infant vesicles, seizures, hepatitis or CSF pleocytosis), not routinely.

verdicts.new_stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to ED at 10 pm with a 4-hour history of a barking cough and noisy breathing. He has a mild coryzal prodrome over the past day and is up to date with immunisations. He is alert and interactive on his mother's lap. He has an intermittent harsh barking cough and audible inspiratory stridor ONLY when agitated. There is no subcostal or suprasternal recession at rest. He swallows saliva easily without drooling. T 37.2 deg C, HR 110, RR 26, SpO2 97 percent on room air. The clinician calculates a Westley score of 1 (mild croup).
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate medication regimen and disposition?

new_options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000 and observation for 4 hours before discharge
new_options.rationale: Adrenaline is for moderate or severe croup with stridor at rest and significant work of breathing; this child has mild croup (Westley 1) and does not need adrenaline.

new_options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg as a single dose, then observe 30 to 60 minutes; if no stridor at rest and well, discharge with written safety-net advice and red flags for return
new_options.rationale: Australian guidance (RCH CPG: Croup, PREDICT) is dexamethasone for ALL croup regardless of severity; for mild croup (Westley 0-2) a single oral dose of 0.15 mg/kg with brief observation is sufficient, with safety-net advice. Adrenaline plus 4-hour observation is reserved for Westley 3 or more.

new_options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg once daily for 3 days
new_options.rationale: Prednisolone is less effective than a single dose of dexamethasone for croup and requires multiple doses; dexamethasone single dose is preferred for its 72-hour duration of effect.

new_options.text: Inhaled budesonide 2 mg via nebuliser as the steroid of choice
new_options.rationale: Nebulised budesonide is an alternative but is more resource-intensive and no more effective than oral dexamethasone, which is first-line.

new_options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days as empirical bacterial cover
new_options.rationale: Croup is viral; antibiotics are not indicated in uncomplicated croup in a fully immunised child.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-month-old female presents in July with 3 days of cough, coryza and difficulty feeding. She was born at term and is fully immunised. Her mother reports she has been feeding about half her normal volume for 12 hours and has had fewer wet nappies. She is alert but irritable with moderate subcostal recessions, audible wheeze, scattered fine end-inspiratory crackles, nasal flaring and no grunting. SpO2 90 percent on room air, RR 60, HR 160, T 37.8 deg C. There is no apnoea witnessed.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which management plan is most appropriate per Australian guidance?

new_options.text: Discharge home with salbutamol MDI plus spacer and GP review in 24 hours
new_options.rationale: Salbutamol is not recommended in bronchiolitis under 12 months; this infant also meets RCH admission criteria (SpO2 below 92 percent persistently, feeding 50 percent of usual).

new_options.text: Admit for supportive care: supplemental oxygen titrated to maintain SpO2 over 92 percent; small frequent feeds or nasogastric or IV fluids if intake is under 50 percent; minimal handling; no bronchodilators or corticosteroids
new_options.rationale: RCH CPG: Bronchiolitis is purely supportive: oxygen for SpO2 persistently under 92 percent (Australian threshold, not 90 percent), hydration support if feeding under 50 percent, minimal handling. Bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics and chest physiotherapy are not beneficial. This infant meets criteria for admission.

new_options.text: Trial nebulised hypertonic 3% saline; if SpO2 improves discharge with weaning plan
new_options.rationale: Hypertonic saline is not recommended for routine bronchiolitis in current Australian or NICE guidance.

new_options.text: Order chest x-ray and FBC; commence IV ampicillin plus gentamicin
new_options.rationale: Routine imaging and broad antibiotics are not indicated in typical bronchiolitis.

new_options.text: Single dose oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and 4-hour observation
new_options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not effective in bronchiolitis; this is not the croup pathway.

verdicts.new_stem: An otherwise healthy 18-month-old boy is brought to ED after a single witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home lasting 2 minutes. His mother reports a 24-hour history of fever to 39.0 deg C with a coryzal illness. Immediately after the seizure he was drowsy but now, 45 minutes later, he is alert, interactive and feeding well. There is no history of previous seizures, no developmental concerns and no family history of epilepsy. On examination he is febrile and alert with clear nasal discharge. Neurological examination is normal with no focal deficits and no meningeal signs. T 38.8 deg C, HR 130, RR 28, SpO2 98 percent. He is fully immunised.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

new_options.text: Perform lumbar puncture to exclude meningitis because febrile seizures under 2 years can mask bacterial meningitis
new_options.rationale: Routine LP after a simple febrile seizure in an immunised, well, neurologically normal child without meningism is not indicated; LP-by-age-alone is the anti-pattern.

new_options.text: Commence oral phenobarbitone 5 mg/kg/day for 12 months as anticonvulsant prophylaxis
new_options.rationale: Anticonvulsant prophylaxis is not recommended; recurrence risk does not justify prophylaxis and it does not alter the risk of later epilepsy.

new_options.text: Reassure (simple febrile seizure: single, generalised, under 15 minutes, full recovery, no neuro deficit, age 6 months to 6 years); identify and treat the febrile focus; provide written first-aid advice and red-flag escalation criteria; no investigations or prophylaxis
new_options.rationale: This is a simple febrile seizure with an identifiable febrile focus. RCH CPG and NICE management is reassurance, treatment of the underlying febrile source, written first-aid advice on positioning and timing for ambulance call (over 5 minutes), and no investigations or prophylaxis. Antipyretics are for comfort, not for seizure prevention.

new_options.text: Urgent CT brain to exclude a structural lesion
new_options.rationale: Neuroimaging is not indicated for simple febrile convulsions with a normal neurological examination.

new_options.text: Prescribe rectal diazepam 0.5 mg/kg for home use as rescue therapy
new_options.rationale: Rescue benzodiazepines are reserved for prolonged or complex febrile seizures, not a first simple febrile seizure.

verdicts.new_stem: An 8-year-old girl presents with 2 weeks of polyuria, polydipsia and weight loss and 24 hours of vomiting, abdominal pain and drowsiness. She is thin, dehydrated and responds only to pain (GCS 11, E2V3M6). She has Kussmaul breathing with RR 36, HR 140, BP 90/55, CRT 3 seconds and very dry mucous membranes. Weight 24 kg (estimated pre-illness 28 kg). VBG: pH 7.05, pCO2 18, HCO3 5. Glucose 28 mmol/L. Ketones over 6 mmol/L. Na 130, K 5.2, urea 10.5, creatinine 90. There is no other significant history.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which initial intravenous fluid plan best matches RCH and ISPAD paediatric DKA guidance?

new_options.text: 20 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 15 minutes, repeat once if still in shock, then maintenance plus deficit replacement at 10 mL/kg/hour using 0.45% saline with potassium
new_options.rationale: 0.45% saline is NOT used in initial paeds DKA rehydration because of cerebral oedema risk from rapid osmolar shifts; 0.9% saline with added potassium is recommended, and rehydration is calculated to even over 48 hours.

new_options.text: 10 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 30 minutes, then calculate fluid deficit (about 7-10 percent of body weight cap) and replace half the deficit plus 24 hours of maintenance in the first 24 hours and the remainder over the next 24 hours, using 0.9% saline plus 40 mmol/L potassium once urine output is confirmed; start insulin at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour at least 1 hour after fluids begin; monitor for cerebral oedema
new_options.rationale: RCH and ISPAD paediatric DKA management uses a 10 mL/kg 0.9% saline bolus over 30-60 minutes (repeat once if shock; AU practice caps total resuscitation boluses to avoid cerebral oedema), then deficit-plus-maintenance over 48 hours with 0.9% saline and potassium once UOP confirmed. Insulin is started no earlier than 1 hour after fluids at 0.05-0.1 units/kg/hour. Cerebral oedema surveillance is mandatory.

new_options.text: Rapid 40 mL/kg bolus of 0.9% saline over 1 hour to restore circulating volume, then replace deficit over 24 hours
new_options.rationale: Large rapid boluses increase cerebral oedema risk; deficit is replaced over 48 hours, not 24 hours.

new_options.text: Commence IV insulin 0.1 units/kg/hour immediately before any fluid bolus, with maintenance 0.9% saline and 5% dextrose
new_options.rationale: Fluid resuscitation precedes insulin; insulin within the first hour increases the risk of cardiovascular collapse and cerebral oedema. Fluids-before-insulin is the AU pattern.

new_options.text: IV 0.9% saline at maintenance rate plus a 10 mL/kg bolus, with IV sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg to correct the acidosis
new_options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate is not routinely recommended in paediatric DKA; it may worsen intracellular acidosis and increase cerebral oedema risk.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-year-old boy with previous mild intermittent croup presents at 2200 with a barking cough and stridor at rest. He has moderate subcostal and suprasternal recessions. SpO2 93 percent on room air, HR 130, RR 32, T 37.4 deg C. The clinician calculates a Westley score of 4 (moderate). He is given oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1:1000. Within 30 minutes his stridor at rest resolves and he looks comfortable. He has been observed for 1 hour. The ED registrar is now considering disposition.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate minimum observation period after the LAST adrenaline dose, and what condition supports safe discharge?

new_options.text: 1 hour after adrenaline, then discharge if stridor-free
new_options.rationale: Insufficient to capture rebound; adrenaline lasts about 2 hours and rebound can occur after that.

new_options.text: 2 hours after adrenaline, then discharge if stridor-free
new_options.rationale: Adrenaline effect lasts up to 2 hours and rebound can occur in the next 1-2 hours, so 2 hours is insufficient.

new_options.text: Minimum 4 hours after the LAST adrenaline dose with the child stridor-free at rest, no recessions, comfortable on room air, and tolerating oral fluids; written safety-net advice and access to representation arrangements before discharge
new_options.rationale: Australian guidance (RCH CPG: Croup, PREDICT) is a minimum 4-hour observation after the LAST adrenaline dose; discharge requires the child to be stridor-free at rest, without recessions, comfortable on room air, tolerating oral intake, and with safe representation arrangements and written safety-net advice.

new_options.text: 6 hours unconditionally regardless of clinical status
new_options.rationale: Six hours is sometimes used if there is clinical concern but is not the standard minimum requirement.

new_options.text: No specific observation period; discharge once stridor-free
new_options.rationale: Without adequate observation rebound stridor can occur at home; observation is required.

verdicts.new_stem: A 22-month-old fully immunised boy is brought to his GP with 12 hours of fever (38.6 deg C) and increasing irritability with right ear pulling. He has had no vomiting and is drinking reasonably. Otoscopy of the right ear shows a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with loss of the light reflex and absent mobility on pneumatic otoscopy; the left tympanic membrane is normal. There is no otorrhoea, no mastoid tenderness and no neck stiffness. He has no risk factors for severe AOM (not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander, no cleft palate, no immunodeficiency, no cochlear implant). His mother asks whether he needs antibiotics or analgesia alone.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate diagnosis and management plan?

new_options.text: Otitis media with effusion; reassurance and review at 6 weeks
new_options.rationale: OME has a retracted or neutral, dull but not bulging tympanic membrane, usually without acute symptoms; this child has an acutely bulging immobile TM with fever.

new_options.text: Acute otitis media; immediate amoxicillin 30 mg/kg three times daily for 5 days because antibiotics are mandatory for any AOM
new_options.rationale: Antibiotics are not mandatory for every AOM in low-risk children over 2; the AU-specific cut-off is bilateral disease, otorrhoea, severe symptoms, age under 2 with bilateral, or high-risk groups.

new_options.text: Acute otitis media in a low-risk child over 6 months with unilateral disease and an intact TM; regular analgesia (paracetamol and/or ibuprofen) plus safety-net delayed prescription (commence amoxicillin 30 mg/kg eight-hourly for 5 days if not improving by 48 hours)
new_options.rationale: Bulging erythematous immobile TM with acute fever meets diagnostic criteria for AOM. Australian guidance (eTG, RCH CPG) for a well, low-risk over-6-month child with unilateral non-perforated AOM is analgesia and watchful waiting or delayed prescription, with immediate antibiotics reserved for under 6 months, bilateral disease in under-2, otorrhoea, severe systemic symptoms, or high-risk groups (Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander, immunocompromised, cochlear implant).

new_options.text: Chronic suppurative otitis media; refer to ENT
new_options.rationale: CSOM requires persistent perforation with discharge for more than 6 weeks; this is acute and the TM is intact.

new_options.text: Otitis externa; commence topical ciprofloxacin drops
new_options.rationale: Otitis externa shows ear canal inflammation with a normal TM; this child has a clearly bulging immobile TM.

verdicts.new_stem: A term infant (birth weight 3.2 kg) is now 4 hours old on the postnatal ward. He is being exclusively breastfed but his mother is concerned he is sleepy and not latching well. Bedside blood glucose is 2.2 mmol/L. He has no clinical signs of hypoglycaemia (no jitteriness, no seizure, no respiratory distress, normal tone). T 36.8 deg C, HR 140, RR 45. He is at risk because of suboptimal feeding.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of immediate management and threshold for escalation is most appropriate per Queensland and ANZNN paediatric guidance?

new_options.text: Support a feed of breast milk (or formula if breast feeding inadequate) AND give 200 mg/kg buccal dextrose 40% gel now; recheck BGL at 30 minutes; if still under 2.6 mmol/L OR any clinical signs of hypoglycaemia develop, escalate to IV 10% dextrose 2 mL/kg bolus and admit for IV maintenance
new_options.rationale: AU/QLD paediatric guidance: for an asymptomatic at-risk infant with BGL under 2.6 mmol/L (the AU threshold), give buccal dextrose 40% gel 200 mg/kg combined with a feed, recheck at 30 minutes, and escalate to IV 10% dextrose for failure to respond or any symptoms. IV dextrose is also first-line if BGL is under 1.5 mmol/L or any symptomatic hypoglycaemia.

new_options.text: Give an IV bolus of 2 mL/kg of 10% dextrose immediately as first-line
new_options.rationale: IV dextrose is for severe (under 1.5 mmol/L) or symptomatic hypoglycaemia, or after failure of feed plus dextrose gel.

new_options.text: Observe and repeat BGL in 2 hours without specific intervention
new_options.rationale: BGL 2.2 mmol/L is below the 2.6 threshold and requires intervention, not passive observation.

new_options.text: Commence IV 10% dextrose maintenance at 60 mL/kg/day without a bolus
new_options.rationale: Maintenance IV is for persistent or severe hypoglycaemia after failure of feed plus dextrose gel.

new_options.text: Administer IM glucagon 0.1 mg/kg as first-line
new_options.rationale: Glucagon is reserved for refractory hypoglycaemia where IV access is delayed or has failed.

verdicts.new_stem: A 13-year-old girl presents with 3 months of bloody diarrhoea, abdominal pain and 6 kg weight loss. She has noticed a painful perianal lump that discharges pus. On examination she is pale, BMI under the 5th centile, with a perianal fistula draining pus and no mouth ulcers. Bloods: Hb 92, CRP 58, albumin 28, faecal calprotectin over 2000 mcg/g. Colonoscopy shows recto-sigmoid inflammation with skip lesions in the caecum and deep serpiginous ulcers in the terminal ileum; biopsies are pending. HR 105, BP 100/65, T 37.3 deg C.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most discriminating endoscopic feature is correct?

new_options.text: Crohn's disease; perianal fistula together with terminal ileal involvement and skip lesions is the discriminating finding
new_options.rationale: Perianal fistula, terminal ileal deep serpiginous ulcers and skip lesions of the colon are characteristic of Crohn's. Ulcerative colitis lacks perianal fistulae, is continuous from the rectum without skip lesions, and does not involve the small bowel. Backwash ileitis in UC affects the caecum only with surface inflammation, not deep serpiginous ulcers.

new_options.text: Ulcerative colitis; perianal fistula reflects ongoing severe colitis
new_options.rationale: UC does not cause perianal fistula and is continuous without skip lesions; perianal fistula essentially excludes UC.

new_options.text: Infectious colitis with secondary perianal abscess
new_options.rationale: Chronicity (3 months), weight loss, anaemia, fistula and the endoscopic pattern point to Crohn's, not acute infection.

new_options.text: Coeliac disease with secondary anaemia
new_options.rationale: Coeliac disease does not produce bloody diarrhoea or perianal fistula.

new_options.text: Behcet disease with intestinal involvement
new_options.rationale: Behcet typically features recurrent oral and genital ulcers (absent here); the endoscopic distribution and perianal fistula favour Crohn's.

verdicts.new_stem: A 5-year-old boy has a 3-week history of lethargy, pallor and multiple unexplained bruises. He complains of leg pain that wakes him at night. On examination he has pallor, multiple bruises and petechiae, with hepatomegaly 3 cm and splenomegaly 2 cm. FBC: Hb 65, WCC 2.8, platelets 22, with occasional blast cells on the film. He is afebrile but tachycardic. There has been no recent infection or medication change.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most discriminating laboratory feature is correct?

new_options.text: Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia; pancytopenia WITH circulating blasts and hepatosplenomegaly together exclude ITP and aplastic anaemia and point to ALL
new_options.rationale: The triad of pancytopenia, circulating blasts on the film, and hepatosplenomegaly with bone pain is characteristic of ALL (the most common childhood malignancy). ITP causes isolated thrombocytopenia. Aplastic anaemia has pancytopenia but NO blasts and NO hepatosplenomegaly. The discriminating feature is the combination, not any one element.

new_options.text: Aplastic anaemia; the pancytopenia is the discriminating feature
new_options.rationale: Aplastic anaemia is pancytopenic but does NOT have circulating blasts or hepatosplenomegaly.

new_options.text: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura; petechiae and bruising are the discriminating features
new_options.rationale: ITP shows ISOLATED thrombocytopenia with normal Hb and WCC and no organomegaly; not this picture.

new_options.text: Osteomyelitis with reactive marrow suppression
new_options.rationale: Osteomyelitis is focal with raised WCC, not pancytopenia with blasts.

new_options.text: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis with secondary marrow involvement
new_options.rationale: JIA causes joint swelling and stiffness, not blasts on the film.

verdicts.new_stem: A 2-year-old boy is brought to a GP by his parents who are concerned about his language. He says only 5 single words, does not combine words, and does not point to share interest. He rarely responds to his name and spends long periods lining up his toy cars. He was born at term and walked at 14 months. His newborn hearing screen was normal. There has been no developmental regression. The GP wishes to start the developmental concern assessment pathway.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of investigation and next-step referral is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Repeat formal audiology assessment AND administer the M-CHAT-R/F autism-specific screening tool; if either is abnormal, refer to a developmental paediatrician or community paediatric service via the public pathway; the AGPAR-funded diagnostic assessment requires a paediatrician or psychologist using DSM-5 criteria
new_options.rationale: Australian autism diagnostic pathway begins with parallel audiology (always exclude hearing loss in a language-delayed child) and an ASD-specific screen (M-CHAT-R/F is the validated tool for 16-30 months). Positive screen plus delayed language triggers referral to a developmental paediatrician or psychologist for a formal multi-disciplinary diagnostic assessment under DSM-5, which is the pathway that unlocks NDIS access.

new_options.text: Administer the Ages and Stages Questionnaire (ASQ-3) alone and reassess at age 3 if delayed
new_options.rationale: ASQ-3 is a general developmental screen and is not autism-specific; in a child with multiple ASD red flags an ASD-specific tool plus audiology is required, and waiting until age 3 delays diagnosis.

new_options.text: Administer the Childhood Autism Rating Scale (CARS) in the GP rooms today and provide a diagnosis
new_options.rationale: CARS is a diagnostic instrument used by trained clinicians as part of multi-disciplinary assessment, not a first-line GP screening tool.

new_options.text: Order WPPSI cognitive assessment to establish intellectual level before any autism workup
new_options.rationale: WPPSI is a cognitive battery, not an ASD screen; cognitive assessment is part of the later multi-disciplinary diagnostic assessment, not the GP screening step.

new_options.text: Refer directly to a developmental paediatrician without screening because referrals are accepted on clinical concern alone
new_options.rationale: Documenting an autism-specific screen and audiology result strengthens the referral and ensures correct triage and NDIS pathway eligibility; direct referral without screen and audiology is not best practice.

verdicts.new_stem: A 5-month-old female with a 3-day history of cough and coryza presents in July. She has been feeding poorly, taking only half her normal volume over 12 hours. She is alert with moderate subcostal and intercostal recessions, audible wheeze and fine crackles. SpO2 90 percent on room air, RR 58, HR 160, T 37.8 deg C. She has had no apnoeas. She is fully immunised for age and has no congenital heart disease or chronic lung disease.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan per RCH bronchiolitis CPG?

new_options.text: Discharge home with nasal saline drops and small frequent feeds; GP review at 24 hours
new_options.rationale: This infant meets RCH admission criteria (SpO2 persistently under 92 percent, feeding 50 percent of usual); discharge is unsafe.

new_options.text: Admit for supportive care: supplemental oxygen titrated to maintain SpO2 over 92 percent; small frequent oral feeds or nasogastric or IV fluids if intake remains under 50 percent; close observation; no bronchodilators, no corticosteroids, no routine antibiotics
new_options.rationale: Moderate bronchiolitis with hypoxia (SpO2 under 92 percent on room air) and feeding under 50 percent meets RCH admission criteria; supportive care (oxygen, hydration, observation) is the mainstay. Bronchodilators, steroids and antibiotics are not beneficial.

new_options.text: Trial nebulised salbutamol; if SpO2 improves discharge with a salbutamol weaning plan
new_options.rationale: Salbutamol has no proven benefit in bronchiolitis under 12 months.

new_options.text: Order chest x-ray and FBC, then commence IV antibiotics while awaiting results
new_options.rationale: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis; routine imaging and broad antibiotics are not indicated.

new_options.text: Single dose oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg and observe for 4 hours; if feeding improves, discharge home
new_options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not effective in bronchiolitis.

verdicts.new_stem: A term female infant is born to a 32-year-old mother with SLE and known anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies. At 2 hours of age, an annular erythematous rash is noted on the face and scalp. Heart rate is 70 bpm and regular; she is pink, well-perfused and in no respiratory distress. ECG shows third-degree AV block with atrial rate 140, ventricular rate 70 and narrow QRS. Bedside echo shows normal cardiac anatomy and good biventricular function; there is no hydrops. BP 65/35, SpO2 96 percent on room air. There are no clinical signs of low cardiac output.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of immediate management and longer-term plan is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Empirical IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg to clear maternal autoantibodies and reverse the block
new_options.rationale: IVIG does not reverse established complete heart block in neonatal lupus; antibody-mediated injury to the conduction system is permanent once block is established.

new_options.text: Admit for continuous cardiac monitoring with intensive care input; do NOT pace acutely because the infant is asymptomatic with adequate ventricular escape; refer to paediatric cardiology for serial assessment and permanent pacemaker if ventricular rate falls under 50-55 bpm, signs of low cardiac output develop, or evolving cardiomyopathy is seen; counsel parents that the block is permanent and that the skin rash will resolve as maternal antibodies clear over 6 months
new_options.rationale: Anti-Ro/SSA mediated complete heart block is permanent. In an asymptomatic neonate with a ventricular rate over 55 bpm and normal function, immediate management is monitoring; permanent pacing is reserved for symptomatic bradycardia, low cardiac output, evolving cardiomyopathy, or ventricular rate under 50-55 bpm. The skin lesions resolve as maternal antibodies clear (around 6 months), per RCH CPG and AAP guidance.

new_options.text: Oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg/day to suppress autoimmune injury to the conduction system
new_options.rationale: Postnatal steroids do not reverse established complete heart block; this is not standard for an asymptomatic infant.

new_options.text: Observe without specific monitoring; the block typically resolves in 6 months as maternal antibodies clear
new_options.rationale: The block is permanent; only the skin rash resolves with antibody clearance.

new_options.text: Insert a temporary transvenous pacemaker immediately to target a rate of 100 bpm
new_options.rationale: Temporary pacing is reserved for symptomatic bradycardia or ventricular rates under 50 bpm with haemodynamic compromise.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-month-old female presents to ED with fever (38.6 deg C), irritability and poor feeding for 24 hours. She has no cough, rash or focal infection. Bag urine on the ward showed nitrites positive, leukocyte esterase 3+ and white cells over 100 x 10^6/L. The on-call paediatric registrar is reviewing the next step. The infant is irritable, has a normal fontanelle, and HR 170, RR 44, SpO2 97 percent on room air.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of confirmatory sampling, empirical therapy and imaging is most appropriate per RCH guidance?

new_options.text: Commence oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole now based on the bag urine and arrange outpatient renal ultrasound
new_options.rationale: Bag urine has high contamination rates and is screening only; under 3 months, hospital admission for parenteral antibiotics is required and a sterile specimen (catheter or SPA) is needed before antibiotics.

new_options.text: Obtain a sterile specimen by catheter (or SPA) for urine culture before antibiotics; admit and commence IV benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus IV gentamicin; arrange renal tract ultrasound during admission and DMSA or MCUG only if indicated post-admission
new_options.rationale: RCH CPG: UTI requires a STERILE specimen (catheter or suprapubic aspirate) before treatment when bag urine is suggestive, because bag urine can over-diagnose and contaminate cultures. For febrile UTI in an infant under 3 months, admission with IV ampicillin plus gentamicin is recommended (avoiding ceftriaxone in neonates), with renal tract ultrasound during admission to detect structural abnormality; DMSA or MCUG are considered later based on results.

new_options.text: Trust the bag urine and commence IV antibiotics immediately without further sampling
new_options.rationale: Bag urine has too high a false-positive rate to direct treatment; sterile sampling is required.

new_options.text: Treat with antipyretics and review in 24 hours assuming viral illness
new_options.rationale: The urinalysis strongly suggests UTI; untreated infant UTI risks pyelonephritis and renal scarring.

new_options.text: Perform lumbar puncture before any antibiotics
new_options.rationale: LP is not routinely required for isolated UTI in a well-appearing infant aged 3 months; it is considered if sepsis or meningitis is otherwise suspected.

verdicts.new_stem: A 9-month-old boy presents with intermittent colicky abdominal pain, red-currant-jelly stool and a palpable sausage-shaped mass in the right upper quadrant. Ultrasound confirms an ileocolic intussusception. He is haemodynamically stable (HR 150, BP 85/55, RR 36, T 37.2 deg C), afebrile and has a soft abdomen without peritonism. IV fluid resuscitation is running. Abdominal x-ray shows no pneumoperitoneum. There is no evidence of small-bowel perforation. Paediatric surgery is on standby. The radiology team is available for fluoroscopic-guided reduction.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive management and what is the safety prerequisite for that procedure?

new_options.text: Non-surgical reduction by air enema under fluoroscopic guidance, performed with paediatric surgery on standby and IV access and fluid resuscitation in place; only proceed if peritonitis, perforation, or refractory shock are ABSENT
new_options.rationale: In a stable child without peritonitis or perforation, air-contrast enema reduction is first-line in Australia, with paediatric surgery on standby in case of perforation or failed reduction. Contraindications (peritonitis, pneumoperitoneum, refractory shock) must be absent; attempting enema in those circumstances is the anti-pattern. Surgical reduction is reserved for failed enema, contraindications, or pathological lead point.

new_options.text: Emergency laparotomy with manual reduction as first-line
new_options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for failed enema, perforation, or peritonitis.

new_options.text: Observation with serial abdominal examinations
new_options.rationale: Untreated intussusception does not reliably resolve and risks ischaemia and perforation.

new_options.text: Diagnostic laparoscopy and laparoscopic reduction as first-line
new_options.rationale: Surgical (open or laparoscopic) reduction is second-line in Australia, after a failed enema attempt or when contraindicated.

new_options.text: IV antibiotics and bowel rest
new_options.rationale: Mechanical reduction is required; antibiotics alone do not reduce the intussusception.

verdicts.new_stem: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the ED by her parents after they discovered she ingested approximately 50 tablets of paracetamol 500 mg (total 25 g) following an argument at school. The ingestion occurred approximately 10 hours ago. She complains of nausea and mild right upper quadrant discomfort. She weighs 45 kg. She is alert and oriented with slight epigastric tenderness. HR 100, BP 105/65, RR 22, SpO2 99 percent, T 36.9 deg C. A paracetamol level drawn 9 hours post-ingestion is 1800 micromol/L. ALT 45 U/L, AST 50 U/L, total bilirubin 18, INR 1.1, creatinine 60. She has no known medical history and no other ingestions.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

new_options.text: Commence IV N-acetylcysteine using the AU two-bag regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours) without waiting for the nomogram-line comparison, given the dose ingested is well above 200 mg/kg; monitor LFTs and INR at completion and continue NAC if hepatotoxicity is evolving
new_options.rationale: Australian guidance (RCH CPG, TG Toxicology) uses a paracetamol level plotted against the Rumack-Matthew nomogram for single acute ingestions 4-15 hours post-ingestion; her 9-hour level of 1800 micromol/L is well above the treatment line. AU practice has transitioned to a two-bag NAC regimen (200 mg/kg over 4 hours then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours) which reduces anaphylactoid reactions vs the older three-bag protocol. Treatment is started immediately and NAC is continued if LFTs or INR show hepatotoxicity at the end of the initial course.

new_options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g orally now, then reassess the need for NAC
new_options.rationale: Activated charcoal is effective only within 1-2 hours of ingestion; at 10 hours it has no role.

new_options.text: Observe without treatment as ALT and INR are currently normal; repeat bloods at 12 hours to detect delayed hepatotoxicity
new_options.rationale: Normal initial ALT does not exclude impending hepatotoxicity; the level is well above the nomogram line and NAC must be started promptly.

new_options.text: Refer for urgent liver transplantation evaluation given the high paracetamol level
new_options.rationale: Transplantation is for established fulminant failure unresponsive to NAC, not initial presentation with normal LFTs.

new_options.text: IV pantoprazole 40 mg for gastric protection and immediate psychiatric review
new_options.rationale: Psychiatric review is essential after medical stabilisation, but the immediate priority is NAC to prevent liver damage.

verdicts.new_stem: A 7-year-old girl with genetic generalised epilepsy presents with convulsive status epilepticus. She received buccal midazolam 0.5 mg/kg 20 minutes ago without effect, then IV levetiracetam 40 mg/kg 15 minutes ago with ongoing seizures. She has now been convulsing continuously for 45 minutes. IV access is established and she is intubated and ventilated by the ED team. BP 90/55, HR 130, SpO2 96 percent. BGL 6.0, Na 138, ionised calcium normal. CT brain performed earlier is normal. There is no eclampsia, no hyponatraemia and no hypoglycaemia. The paediatric neurologist is unavailable and PICU is preparing to admit.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next anticonvulsant under the APLS Australia status epilepticus pathway?

new_options.text: IV midazolam infusion 0.1-0.2 mg/kg/hour titrated to seizure suppression with PICU monitoring; consider thiopentone or phenobarbitone if midazolam infusion is ineffective
new_options.rationale: Following first-line benzodiazepines and a second-line agent (levetiracetam), refractory paediatric convulsive status epilepticus is managed with a benzodiazepine infusion (midazolam) or thiopentone or phenobarbitone, in PICU with continuous EEG where available. Midazolam infusion is the most commonly used step at this point in AU paediatric ICUs.

new_options.text: IV phenytoin 20 mg/kg over 20 minutes as third-line
new_options.rationale: She has already had levetiracetam as a second-line sodium-channel/glutamate-modulating agent; a third dose of a similar second-line agent is less likely to be effective at this stage, and the AU pathway escalates to a benzodiazepine infusion or anaesthetic.

new_options.text: IV propofol 2 mg/kg bolus then prolonged infusion at 4 mg/kg/hour
new_options.rationale: Propofol infusion is generally avoided in children for prolonged sedation because of propofol-infusion syndrome.

new_options.text: Intramuscular paraldehyde 0.4 mL/kg
new_options.rationale: Paraldehyde is rarely used now and is not recommended when IV access is available.

new_options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 50 mg/kg
new_options.rationale: Magnesium is reserved for eclampsia or hypomagnesaemia, neither present here.

verdicts.new_stem: A 4-week-old male infant with projectile non-bilious vomiting has ultrasound-confirmed hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. He was clinically dehydrated on arrival. Initial VBG: pH 7.52, pCO2 46, HCO3 34, Cl 82, Na 130, K 3.2. He received 0.9% saline 20 mL/kg bolus and is now on 0.9% saline plus 5% dextrose plus 40 mmol/L potassium chloride at maintenance. Twenty-four hours later: Cl 96, HCO3 27, urine output adequate. The surgical registrar is preparing for Ramstedt pyloromyotomy and the anaesthetist is reviewing operative readiness.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which biochemical thresholds must be met before proceeding to pyloromyotomy?

new_options.text: Chloride over 95 mmol/L AND bicarbonate under 30 mmol/L AND potassium over 3.5 mmol/L (with adequate urine output)
new_options.rationale: The AU preoperative thresholds for pyloromyotomy in HPS are Cl over 95 (often quoted over 100), HCO3 under 30 and K over 3.5 mmol/L with adequate UOP. Operating before correction increases perioperative apnoea risk from persistent CSF alkalosis and risks arrhythmia from hypokalaemia, per RCH CPG: Pyloric Stenosis.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Paediatric Fluid and Electrolyte

new_options.text: Sodium over 135 mmol/L and potassium over 3.5 mmol/L only
new_options.rationale: Sodium and potassium normalisation matter but the primary indices of alkalosis correction are chloride and bicarbonate.

new_options.text: Venous pH below 7.40 only
new_options.rationale: pH may normalise before the chloride deficit is fully corrected; chloride and bicarbonate are the specific indices.

new_options.text: Base excess below +5 only
new_options.rationale: Base excess tracks bicarbonate but does not address the chloride deficit specifically.

new_options.text: Normal serum creatinine alone
new_options.rationale: Creatinine reflects dehydration but is not the key operative-readiness parameter.

verdicts.new_stem: A 7-year-old boy with new-onset T1DM is being treated for severe DKA with IV insulin 0.1 units/kg/hr and IV 0.9% saline. Initial: pH 6.98, HCO3 4, glucose 32, ketones over 6. After 4 hours: glucose 18, pH 7.12, HCO3 7, ketones 4.5. He has received 1.5 L of 0.9% saline. He suddenly develops a severe headache, becomes bradycardic (HR 55), hypertensive (BP 130/90), and his GCS drops from 15 to 9. Pupils are equal but sluggish. Na 130, K 4.8. Glucose remains 18 (NOT hypoglycaemia).
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

new_options.text: Administer IV 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 10-20 minutes (preferred in normonatraemia or hyponatraemia) OR IV mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg over 20 minutes; reduce the IV fluid rate by approximately one-third; nurse head midline with 30-degree head-up; arrange urgent CT brain and PICU input; do NOT routinely hyperventilate
new_options.rationale: The triad of headache, bradycardia, hypertension and falling GCS during DKA treatment is the Cushing reflex of cerebral oedema. Australian and ISPAD guidance is immediate osmotherapy (3 percent saline 5 mL/kg or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg), fluid rate reduction by about a third, head elevation, urgent imaging and PICU transfer. Routine hyperventilation is avoided because of cerebral ischaemia risk.

new_options.text: Stop insulin, give 2 mL/kg of 50% dextrose IV and IV hydrocortisone 50 mg
new_options.rationale: Glucose is 18 mmol/L (not hypoglycaemia) and there is no adrenal crisis; cerebral oedema requires osmotherapy.

new_options.text: Intubate immediately and hyperventilate to pCO2 25-30 mmHg to lower intracranial pressure
new_options.rationale: Routine hyperventilation is not recommended; brief hyperventilation only as a temporising measure during herniation. Osmotherapy is the immediate intervention.

new_options.text: Increase 0.9% saline rate to 20 mL/kg/hour to improve cerebral perfusion
new_options.rationale: Rapid fluid administration can worsen cerebral oedema; the rate should be REDUCED, not increased.

new_options.text: Reduce insulin to 0.05 units/kg/hour and continue current management
new_options.rationale: Insulin reduction alone does not address cerebral oedema; immediate osmotherapy is required.

verdicts.new_stem: A 16-year-old girl with severe anorexia nervosa and BMI 14 presents with 4 weeks of postprandial epigastric pain, early satiety and bilious vomiting; she has lost a further 3 kg. Examination: scaphoid abdomen, visible aortic pulsation and succussion splash. Upper GI contrast: dilated proximal duodenum with abrupt linear cut-off at the third part. CT angiography: aortomesenteric angle 6 degrees (normal 28-65). She is haemodynamically stable. BP 90/60, HR 100. She has no other identified GI disease.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of diagnosis and management plan is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Superior mesenteric artery (SMA) syndrome from severe weight loss; immediate management is nasojejunal feeding (post-obstruction enteric route) with cautious refeeding under a paediatric eating-disorder team to avoid refeeding syndrome, electrolyte monitoring (phosphate, magnesium, potassium), and surgical duodenojejunostomy reserved for failure of medical and nutritional restoration
new_options.rationale: SMA syndrome (Wilkie's) occurs when the aortomesenteric angle is reduced (often under 25 degrees) compressing the third part of the duodenum, typically after rapid weight loss as in anorexia nervosa. AU paediatric multi-step management: nasojejunal feeds (bypass the obstruction) with structured refeeding under MARSIPAN-AU principles (electrolyte and refeeding-syndrome surveillance), with surgery (duodenojejunostomy) reserved for failure to gain weight or anatomical persistence.

new_options.text: Chronic duodenal atresia
new_options.rationale: Duodenal atresia presents in the neonatal period with bilious vomiting on day 1.

new_options.text: Annular pancreas
new_options.rationale: Annular pancreas typically presents in infancy or early childhood.

new_options.text: Eosinophilic oesophagitis
new_options.rationale: EoE presents with dysphagia and food impaction, not duodenal-level obstruction with the radiologic cut-off described.

new_options.text: Median arcuate ligament (coeliac artery compression) syndrome
new_options.rationale: MALS causes coeliac artery compression and post-prandial epigastric pain but does not cause the duodenal cut-off seen here.

verdicts.new_stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to ED after being struck by a car. He was intubated on scene for GCS 5 (E1V2M2) with a left fixed dilated pupil. On arrival BP 85/50, HR 140, SpO2 98 percent on ventilation. Non-contrast CT shows a large left subdural haematoma with 8 mm midline shift, effaced basal cisterns and early uncal herniation. The neurosurgical team is 20 minutes away by retrieval. He has decerebrate posturing on the right and the left pupil remains fixed at 6 mm.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which medical intervention is most appropriate to reduce ICP while awaiting transfer, AND why is it preferred over the alternative osmotherapy in this context?

new_options.text: IV 3% hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 10-20 minutes (preferred over mannitol because of his hypotension - hypertonic saline expands intravascular volume whereas mannitol causes osmotic diuresis), positioning head midline with 30-degree elevation, normocapnia, treat hypotension to maintain CPP, brief mild hyperventilation only for acute herniation signs while awaiting neurosurgery
new_options.rationale: BTF paediatric and RCH guidance: in a child with herniation signs AND hypotension, hypertonic saline is preferred over mannitol because mannitol can worsen the hypotension via osmotic diuresis. Hypertonic saline reduces ICP and expands intravascular volume. Head positioning, normocapnia and CPP maintenance are concurrent measures; brief mild hyperventilation is only a temporising rescue for active herniation.

new_options.text: IV mannitol 1 g/kg over 20 minutes
new_options.rationale: Mannitol is effective osmotherapy but causes osmotic diuresis and can worsen hypotension; hypertonic saline is preferred in shock.

new_options.text: Immediately hyperventilate to pCO2 25-30 mmHg as the primary intervention
new_options.rationale: Prophylactic aggressive hyperventilation can cause cerebral ischaemia and is not recommended; osmotherapy is first-line.

new_options.text: Insert ICP monitor and start barbiturate (thiopentone) coma immediately
new_options.rationale: ICP monitor and barbiturate coma are later steps after initial stabilisation, neurosurgical consultation, and definitive surgical decompression as appropriate.

new_options.text: IV dexamethasone 0.5 mg/kg to reduce cerebral oedema
new_options.rationale: Steroids are not indicated in TBI and may worsen outcomes.

verdicts.new_stem: A 14-year-old girl with ileocolonic Crohn's disease has been in clinical remission on maintenance infliximab 5 mg/kg every 8 weeks plus azathioprine 1.5 mg/kg/day. Over the past 3 months she has developed abdominal pain, 4-5 loose stools per day and a rising faecal calprotectin from 50 to 850 mcg/g. She is due for her infliximab infusion today. Pre-infusion bloods: infliximab trough under 1 mcg/mL (therapeutic over 3), anti-infliximab antibodies (ATI) HIGH at 120 ng/mL, CRP 35, albumin 30. There is no perianal disease and no surgical emergency. She has no contraindications to anti-TNF or methotrexate.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step given the therapeutic drug monitoring result?

new_options.text: Switch to adalimumab (second anti-TNF) with loading 160 mg / 80 mg at 2 weeks / 40 mg every 2 weeks and ADD an immunomodulator (methotrexate or continued azathioprine) to reduce the risk of immunogenicity against the new biologic; monitor trough levels and ATI
new_options.rationale: ECCO/ESPGHAN and AU IBD practice: subtherapeutic trough with HIGH ATI indicates immunogenic loss of response; dose escalation will not overcome neutralising antibodies. The recommended step is to switch within class (to adalimumab) and add or continue an immunomodulator (methotrexate or azathioprine) to prevent immunogenicity against the second anti-TNF.

new_options.text: Increase infliximab to 10 mg/kg every 4 weeks
new_options.rationale: Dose escalation is appropriate for low trough without significant ATI; with HIGH neutralising antibodies, escalation is unlikely to work and risks infusion reactions.

new_options.text: Switch to ustekinumab as a first-line out-of-class agent without trying a second anti-TNF
new_options.rationale: Ustekinumab is a valid alternative, but AU practice typically reserves out-of-class switching until after failure of a second anti-TNF unless specific contraindications to a second anti-TNF exist.

new_options.text: Add oral prednisolone 40 mg daily and continue the same infliximab regimen
new_options.rationale: Steroids may bridge symptoms but do not address the underlying immunogenic loss of response.

new_options.text: Stop all biologic therapy and refer for elective colectomy
new_options.rationale: Surgery is reserved for medically refractory or complicated disease; multiple medical options remain.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-year-old boy with trisomy 21 (AVSD repair at 6 months, normal TSH 5 months ago, fully immunised) is brought to the GP with 3 weeks of progressive lethargy, intermittent low-grade fevers without an obvious source, and new bruising over shins and trunk that he does not recall sustaining. He has been on no medications and has had no preceding viral illness. On examination he is pale, has a smooth firm spleen palpable 3 cm below the left costal margin, generalised non-tender cervical lymphadenopathy with the largest node 2 cm, multiple bruises in non-traumatic distribution and palatal petechiae. He is haemodynamically stable. Capillary FBC: Hb 68, WCC 22 with blasts on film, platelets 28. He is not febrile to a level requiring urgent neutropenic sepsis protocol but the GP wants the appropriate disposition.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Reassure as likely viral illness and repeat FBC in 2 weeks
new_options.rationale: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts plus hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy is leukaemia until proven otherwise; delay is unsafe.

new_options.text: Empirical oral antibiotics for presumed occult bacterial infection and review at 48 hours
new_options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics without a focus and without acknowledging the haematological abnormality delays diagnosis (anti-pattern: treating symptoms without addressing marrow failure).

new_options.text: Arrange immediate (same-day) transfer to a tertiary paediatric oncology service for marrow assessment, supportive care and isolation; involve haematology-oncology and confirm trisomy 21-specific monitoring including TMD/ML-DS pathway awareness
new_options.rationale: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts, hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy in a child with trisomy 21 is leukaemia until proven otherwise; T21 carries an approximately 10-20-fold increased risk of both ALL and AMKL (and a distinct ML-DS pathway). Same-day tertiary transfer for bone marrow biopsy, supportive care (transfusion as needed, neutropenic precautions) and oncology input is mandatory.

new_options.text: Order ferritin, B12 and folate to investigate the anaemia in primary care
new_options.rationale: Nutritional workup does not explain the thrombocytopenia, blasts, splenomegaly or lymphadenopathy.

new_options.text: Refer to outpatient general paediatrics for next available clinic
new_options.rationale: Outpatient referral risks dangerous delay; this child needs same-day tertiary oncology assessment.

verdicts.new_stem: A 4-year-old previously well girl is brought to her GP because her mother has noticed an increasing number of pale brown skin patches over 2 years. There is no family history of neurofibromatosis or unexplained tumours. She is developmentally appropriate, fully immunised and otherwise well. On full examination today she has 7 well-demarcated cafe-au-lait macules across her trunk and limbs, all greater than 5 mm in longest diameter, and bilateral axillary freckling clearly visible. She has no Lisch nodules visible on bedside ophthalmoscopy, no plexiform neurofibroma, no cutaneous neurofibroma, no symptoms of optic glioma, and no skeletal dysplasia. Growth is on the 50th centile (head circumference 75th). Vision and developmental screening today are normal.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate conclusion-and-next-step pair under the 2021 revised NIH/NF1 criteria?

new_options.text: She does not meet criteria because skin findings alone can never be diagnostic of NF1; do not refer
new_options.rationale: The 2021 revised criteria allow diagnosis based on two features and explicitly include 6 or more cafe-au-lait macules plus skin-fold freckling in the absence of a family history.

new_options.text: She meets criteria only if molecular NF1 gene testing returns a pathogenic variant; do not diagnose without sequencing
new_options.rationale: Molecular confirmation is one of the diagnostic criteria but not required when two clinical criteria are present.

new_options.text: She does not meet criteria because the cafe-au-lait macule threshold is at least 10
new_options.rationale: The diagnostic threshold is 6 or more macules over 5 mm prepubertally (or over 15 mm post-pubertally).

new_options.text: She meets criteria (6 or more cafe-au-lait macules over 5 mm prepubertally PLUS skin-fold axillary freckling = two of the 2021 criteria); refer to paediatric genetics and arrange RCH NF1 surveillance pathway including paediatric ophthalmology slit lamp for Lisch nodules and baseline optic pathway screening as per AU surveillance schedule
new_options.rationale: Under the 2021 revised criteria she meets two features (macule count/size and skin-fold freckling). AU practice (RCH CPG: NF1) is then to refer for genetics, formal paediatric ophthalmology slit-lamp (Lisch nodules and optic pathway glioma screening), blood pressure monitoring annually (phaeochromocytoma/renovascular risk) and structured developmental and learning surveillance.

new_options.text: She meets criteria only if a first-degree relative also has clinically confirmed NF1
new_options.rationale: Affected first-degree relative is one of several alternative features but not required when other clinical features are present.

verdicts.new_stem: A 34-year-old G2P1 woman is referred to tertiary foetal medicine after a 28-week scan showed a 14 mm well-circumscribed echogenic mass on the foetal interventricular septum and a second 8 mm mass on the LV free wall. Foetal cardiac function is preserved, there is no hydrops, no significant outflow obstruction and normal amniotic fluid volume. The woman has no personal history of seizures, facial angiofibromas or hypopigmented macules, and a normal renal ultrasound last year. There is no family history of tuberous sclerosis. Dedicated reassessment of the foetal brain, kidneys and skeleton shows no other anomalies (acknowledging antenatal imaging limits).
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of counselling, antenatal investigation and postnatal plan is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Multiple foetal cardiac rhabdomyomas are strongly associated with tuberous sclerosis complex (around 75-80 percent of foetuses with multiple rhabdomyomas have TSC); arrange foetal MRI for cortical tubers and subependymal nodules; counsel that the rhabdomyomas typically regress after birth but TSC requires lifelong multisystem surveillance; arrange postnatal clinical genetics review and TSC1/TSC2 sequencing
new_options.rationale: International TSC consensus: multiple foetal cardiac rhabdomyomas confer about 75-80 percent risk of TSC. Antenatal workup includes foetal MRI for CNS lesions; postnatal: clinical genetics, TSC1/TSC2 sequencing, dermatologic, ophthalmologic, neurology, renal and cardiac surveillance per RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Cardiac rhabdomyomas are almost always isolated benign tumours unrelated to a syndromic diagnosis; no further evaluation required
new_options.rationale: Although lesions regress, multiple rhabdomyomas are strongly associated with TSC and require workup.

new_options.text: Antenatal cardiac rhabdomyoma is essentially diagnostic of Williams syndrome; arrange 7q11.23 microarray
new_options.rationale: Williams syndrome causes supravalvular aortic stenosis and elfin facies, not cardiac rhabdomyomas.

new_options.text: Antenatal cardiac rhabdomyoma is most commonly associated with Noonan syndrome (RASopathy); arrange a RASopathy panel
new_options.rationale: Noonan cardiac involvement is dominated by pulmonary valve stenosis and HCM, not rhabdomyomas.

new_options.text: Lesions typically grow rapidly after birth and require surgical excision in the neonatal period
new_options.rationale: Rhabdomyomas typically peak in the third trimester and regress after birth; surgery is reserved for haemodynamic obstruction or refractory arrhythmia.

verdicts.new_stem: A 5-day-old term male neonate is reviewed on the postnatal ward after a 90-second generalised tonic-clonic seizure that terminated spontaneously. He was born at 38+5 weeks by uncomplicated NVD. Antenatal scans identified interrupted aortic arch type B for which he is scheduled for repair next week. Examination shows hypertelorism, broad nasal bridge, low-set ears with deficient ear lobes, and a small mouth with high-arched palate. The postnatal CXR shows no clear thymic shadow. Investigations: ionised calcium 0.78 mmol/L (low), magnesium 0.85 (normal), glucose 3.4, PTH inappropriately low for the calcium. There has been no maternal calcium-altering medication exposure.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of first-line genetic investigation and live-vaccine planning is most appropriate?

new_options.text: Chromosomal microarray (CMA) as first-line genetic test to detect the 22q11.2 microdeletion (more sensitive than FISH for atypical deletion sizes); withhold live vaccines (rotavirus, BCG, MMR, varicella) until T-cell subsets confirm adequate cellular immunity; commence IV calcium replacement and engage cardiothoracic, immunology, endocrinology and clinical genetics teams
new_options.rationale: The combination of conotruncal cardiac anomaly (IAA-B), hypocalcaemia with inappropriately low PTH, suspected thymic hypoplasia and characteristic facies is the classic 22q11.2 deletion (DiGeorge) phenotype. CMA is first-line and detects atypical deletion sizes that FISH may miss. Live vaccines must be withheld until cellular immunity is confirmed by T-cell subsets, per Australian Immunisation Handbook and RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Standard karyotype looking for trisomy 22
new_options.rationale: 22q11.2 deletion is sub-microscopic and is not detected by standard karyotype; CMA or targeted FISH is required.

new_options.text: Repeat newborn screening card for congenital hypothyroidism
new_options.rationale: Does not address the syndromic combination of cardiac anomaly, hypocalcaemia and thymic hypoplasia.

new_options.text: Maternal vitamin D level only
new_options.rationale: Maternal vitamin D deficiency can cause neonatal hypocalcaemia but does not explain the cardiac anomaly, thymic absence and facies.

new_options.text: Trans-fontanelle cranial ultrasound at the cotside as the next investigation
new_options.rationale: Cranial ultrasound does not investigate the unifying syndromic diagnosis.

verdicts.new_stem: A 22-month-old boy is brought to his GP because of pallor. He was born at 39 weeks (3.4 kg) and was breastfed until 11 months, transitioning to full-cream cow's milk. He drinks about 900 mL of cow's milk per day from a bottle, including overnight, and refuses red meat, eggs and most green vegetables. He passes soft stool daily with no blood, has had no recurrent infections, no bruising and no overseas travel. He is developmentally appropriate (walked 13 months, about 20 words). Examination: alert, well, height and weight on the 50th centile, conjunctival pallor, no jaundice, no organomegaly, no purpura. HR 118, RR 28, T 36.8, SpO2 99 percent on room air. Bloods: Hb 80 g/L, MCV 62, MCH 19, RDW 18 percent, platelets 480, WCC 8.2, ferritin 6, CRP under 5, reticulocytes 1.0 percent.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

new_options.text: Cap cow's milk at 500 mL/day, increase dietary iron-rich foods, AND commence oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day; recheck Hb at 4 weeks (expect a rise of at least 10 g/L) and continue for 3 months after Hb normalises to replete stores
new_options.rationale: Iron deficiency anaemia from excess cow's milk: low ferritin, microcytic anaemia with high RDW and mild thrombocytosis. RCH CPG guidance: dietary modification (cap cow's milk at 500 mL/day) PLUS oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day, with a 4-week response check, continuing for 3 months after Hb normalises to replete stores.

new_options.text: Refer for inpatient packed red cell transfusion because Hb is under 90 g/L
new_options.rationale: He is haemodynamically stable and asymptomatic; transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia or Hb under 70 g/L with cardiovascular compromise.

new_options.text: Order haemoglobin electrophoresis and HbA2 quantification before any treatment
new_options.rationale: Ferritin is unequivocally low and dietary history fits iron deficiency; haemoglobinopathy screening is considered only after iron therapy fails or based on family or ethnic background.

new_options.text: Day-stay IV iron polymaltose infusion now
new_options.rationale: IV iron in a toddler is reserved for malabsorption, oral intolerance, or non-response to oral therapy.

new_options.text: Multivitamin containing 5 mg elemental iron daily and continue current milk intake
