new_options.rationale: Multivitamin iron content is inadequate (5 mg total vs required 3-6 mg/kg/day, which is approximately 36-72 mg for this 12 kg toddler) and excessive milk perpetuates deficiency.

verdicts.new_stem: A 3-year-old girl is reviewed two weeks after her second culture-proven E coli febrile UTI (fever to 39 deg C) treated with oral trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole per local sensitivities. The first UTI was 4 months ago. She has ongoing functional constipation well controlled with macrogol. She is otherwise well, growth on the 60th centile, with normal developmental milestones and an unremarkable antenatal renal ultrasound. There is no family history of renal disease or VUR. Renal ultrasound after the recent admission shows mildly dilated left renal pelvis (AP diameter 8 mm), no hydroureter, no scarring, no duplex system, and normal bladder wall with no post-void residual. The GP is planning the next investigation.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which next investigation is most appropriate, and when should it be performed?

new_options.text: DMSA scan 4-6 months after the most recent UTI to detect cortical scarring; reserve MCUG for cases with hydroureter, abnormal DMSA, or further breakthrough infections; continue treatment of constipation and bladder habits
new_options.rationale: RCH CPG: UTI and KHA-CARI: after recurrent febrile UTI in a young child, DMSA at 4-6 months post-infection detects persistent scarring (acute inflammation has settled by then). MCUG is invasive and is reserved for hydroureter, abnormal DMSA or breakthrough disease, not first-line. Concurrent constipation management is essential.

new_options.text: MCUG within the next week as the next investigation
new_options.rationale: MCUG (catheterisation, ionising radiation) is not first-line after recurrent UTI in this Australian pathway; DMSA is preferred to stratify further imaging.

new_options.text: Commence trimethoprim 2 mg/kg nightly as long-term prophylaxis without further imaging
new_options.rationale: Prophylaxis is considered after risk stratification (high-grade VUR, significant scarring); empirical prophylaxis without imaging is not first-line.

new_options.text: Refer for urological cystoscopy to evaluate the bladder mucosa
new_options.rationale: Cystoscopy is not indicated in straightforward recurrent UTI without anatomical concerns on ultrasound.

new_options.text: Repeat renal ultrasound in 12 months and otherwise observe
new_options.rationale: After two febrile UTIs deferred imaging misses scarring; DMSA at 4-6 months is recommended.

verdicts.discard_reason: Pure NIP schedule recall (12-month cohort = MMR + MenACWY + 13vPCV). The Australian Immunisation Handbook schedule is a memorised fact and the topic does not naturally support alternative-selection, AU clinical cut-off (other than the schedule itself), anti-pattern, or multi-step decision-making distinct from rote recall. Better replaced by an immunisation-decision question (e.g. catch-up after delay, contraindication risk assessment) rather than salvaged.

verdicts.new_stem: A 5-month-old breastfed boy attends his GP with his father, who has read 'a lot online' and wants to delay the 6-month vaccines. The mother is supportive of vaccination and has asked the father to discuss it with the doctor. The father is hesitant rather than refusing; his main concerns are that 'too many vaccines at once overload the immune system' and that aluminium adjuvants 'cause autism'. The infant is otherwise well, growing on the 50th centile, with no medical issues. He had his 2 and 4-month vaccines without adverse events. There is no household medical neglect and the parents otherwise engage with primary care.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which approach is most consistent with current Australian NCIRS SKAI and RACGP guidance for vaccine hesitancy?

new_options.text: Refuse to provide further GP care until the father agrees to vaccination
new_options.rationale: Withholding general care is unethical and not supported by RACGP or NCIRS; trusted continuity is a key driver of eventual acceptance.

new_options.text: Motivational-interviewing-style consultation: presumptive language, explore and acknowledge concerns, share tailored evidence on immune-overload and aluminium myths, give a clear personal recommendation, offer SKAI resources, schedule a follow-up before the next due dose, and document the discussion; recommend the standard schedule rather than an alternative
new_options.rationale: NCIRS SKAI and RACGP recommend MI-style engagement: presumptive language, exploration of concerns, tailored factual rebuttal of specific myths, a clear clinician recommendation and signposting to reputable resources, with a planned follow-up. Adversarial debate and alternative schedules are less effective.

new_options.text: Notify child protection because vaccine refusal constitutes medical neglect
new_options.rationale: Vaccine hesitancy in an otherwise well-cared-for child is not in itself medical neglect.

new_options.text: Offer a spaced-out schedule giving one vaccine per visit
new_options.rationale: Alternative schedules prolong susceptibility, increase visits and pain, and reinforce the immune-overload misconception; the standard schedule should be recommended.

new_options.text: Refer to the Family Court to compel vaccination
new_options.rationale: Court orders are a last resort when parents are in deadlock and other approaches have failed; the initial step is shared GP-led discussion.

verdicts.new_stem: A 6-week-old, ex-39-week, formula-fed, vaccinated-to-age boy is brought to ED by his mother after a measured rectal temperature of 38.4 deg C at home this morning. He has fed slightly less than usual for 6 hours but has had wet nappies, no vomiting, no diarrhoea, no rash and no cough. Maternal GBS negative at 36 weeks, membranes ruptured 4 hours, discharged day 2 without antibiotics. He was previously well. On arrival he is alert, settles to feed, central capillary refill 2 seconds, no focus on examination (clear chest, soft abdomen, flat fontanelle, clean umbilicus). Recorded temperature in ED is 38.2 deg C. Initial bloods: WCC 9.8, neutrophils 5.2, lymphocytes 3.6, CRP 8. Catheter urinalysis: nitrites negative, leukocytes negative. T 38.2, HR 148, RR 42, SpO2 99 percent. The ED registrar is deciding whether lumbar puncture is required.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Discharge home with paracetamol and open 24-hour GP review
new_options.rationale: All febrile infants under 3 months require investigation; outpatient management without workup is not acceptable regardless of how well they look.

new_options.text: Blood and urine cultures (catheter), lumbar puncture, then commence IV benzylpenicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime; admit
new_options.rationale: The AU age-cut-off for mandatory full septic workup INCLUDING lumbar puncture is under 8 weeks of age (about 56 days) regardless of clinical appearance because low-risk criteria do not exclude bacterial meningitis at this age. At 6 weeks the recommended empirical regimen is benzylpenicillin/ampicillin plus gentamicin plus cefotaxime (Listeria, Gram-negatives, CNS penetration), per RCH CPG.

new_options.text: Blood and urine cultures only and observe without antibiotics pending results
new_options.rationale: Selective workup omitting LP is considered only in well infants 1-3 months meeting all low-risk criteria; under 8 weeks LP is mandatory and empirical antibiotics are not withheld.

new_options.text: Oral amoxicillin and ED review at 24 hours
new_options.rationale: Oral antibiotics are inadequate empirical cover; IV therapy is required.

new_options.text: Chest x-ray and 4 hours observation without further investigation
new_options.rationale: CXR is not routinely indicated without respiratory signs and does not substitute for blood, urine and CSF sampling.

verdicts.new_stem: An 18-month-old boy weighing 11 kg (clinic weight 2 weeks ago 11.5 kg) presents with 36 hours of profuse non-bloody diarrhoea and three episodes of vomiting today. His older sibling had similar symptoms last week. He has been drinking some water but refuses solids. On examination he is irritable but consolable, with sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes, mildly delayed skin recoil, central capillary refill 2-3 seconds, warm peripheries and tears with crying. Triage nappy was dry; the last wet nappy was 6 hours ago. HR 142, RR 28, T 37.4 deg C, BP 96/60. There is no abdominal mass and no peritonism.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of dehydration assessment and immediate rehydration plan is most appropriate?

new_options.text: No clinically detectable dehydration (under 3 percent); oral fluids ad lib at home
new_options.rationale: Multiple Gorelick signs are present (sunken eyes, dry mucosae, delayed skin recoil, reduced urine output), so dehydration is clinically detectable.

new_options.text: Mild to moderate dehydration around 4-6 percent (3-5 Gorelick signs, preserved consciousness/tears/BP, weight deficit 0.5/11.5 kg = about 4.3 percent); commence NGT or oral rehydration solution at 50 mL/kg over 4 hours plus ongoing losses, and reassess at 4 hours for ED discharge vs admission
new_options.rationale: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis classification uses the Gorelick signs (sunken eyes, dry mucosae, delayed skin recoil, reduced UO, mildly prolonged CR) and weight deficit. Mild-moderate dehydration around 4-6 percent is managed with nasogastric ORS at 50 mL/kg over 4 hours (or oral if tolerating), with IV reserved for failure of NG or shock.

new_options.text: Severe dehydration (at least 10 percent) with compensated shock; resuscitate with 20 mL/kg 0.9% saline boluses repeated as needed
new_options.rationale: Severe dehydration would require obtunded mental state, very prolonged CR over 3 seconds, weak pulses, deeply sunken eyes and absent tears; this child does not meet that threshold.

new_options.text: Decompensated hypovolaemic shock from severe dehydration; intubate and resuscitate
new_options.rationale: BP, mental status and central perfusion are preserved; this is not decompensated shock.

new_options.text: Euvolaemic viral illness; reassure and discharge with no rehydration
new_options.rationale: Clinical signs and weight loss demonstrate a deficit; rehydration is required.

verdicts.new_stem: A 5-year-old boy weighing 20 kg is brought to a school sick bay 10 minutes after eating a peanut butter sandwich. He had a mild rash to peanut at age 2. Staff describe him as flushed with widespread urticaria, persistent coughing with audible wheeze, hoarse voice and reported throat tightness. He is anxious but responsive, sitting forward. He has no past history of asthma. HR 138, RR 38, SpO2 94 percent on room air. There is no stridor at rest. His EpiPen Jr 150 microgram is in the school office and an ambulance has been called. He has not received any treatment yet.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate action, including correct adrenaline dose for his weight?

new_options.text: Lay him supine with legs elevated unless respiratory distress dictates sitting; administer IM adrenaline 150 microgram (EpiPen Jr) into the anterolateral mid-thigh because he weighs 20 kg (ASCIA 10-20 kg threshold); be ready to repeat at 5 minutes if no response; high-flow oxygen and IV access if available; continue CPR observation while awaiting paramedics
new_options.rationale: He meets ASCIA criteria for anaphylaxis (skin plus respiratory involvement with airway compromise). IM adrenaline 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) is first-line. ASCIA autoinjector thresholds: 10-20 kg use 150 microgram (EpiPen Jr); over 20 kg use 300 microgram (EpiPen); over 50 kg use 500 microgram (EpiPen 500). At 20 kg he is at the upper edge of the 150 microgram band, so 150 microgram is correct per current ASCIA paediatric thresholds.
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

new_options.text: Salbutamol 6 puffs via spacer first and observe for response before adrenaline
new_options.rationale: Wheeze in anaphylaxis is treated with adrenaline first; delay increases mortality.
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

new_options.text: Oral cetirizine and prednisolone with 1-hour observation
new_options.rationale: Antihistamines and corticosteroids are not first-line for anaphylaxis and do not treat airway or cardiovascular compromise.
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

new_options.text: IM adrenaline 300 microgram (EpiPen) immediately
new_options.rationale: 300 microgram is used over 20 kg under ASCIA thresholds; at 20 kg the 150 microgram device is appropriate.

new_options.text: Withhold adrenaline as he is talking and responsive; await paramedic arrival
new_options.rationale: Anaphylaxis with airway compromise (hoarse voice, throat tightness) requires IM adrenaline now.
source_refs: ASCIA Guidelines for acute management of anaphylaxis

verdicts.new_stem: A previously well 18-month-old boy weighing 11 kg presents to ED after a single 3-minute generalised tonic-clonic seizure at home. He had been febrile to 39.0 deg C with coryzal symptoms for 24 hours. He recovered fully within 20 minutes. On examination he is alert and playful with a clear viral URTI and no other source of infection. There is no rash, no meningism and no focal neurology. He has no past seizure history; his father had two febrile seizures as a toddler. He is fully immunised, has not received sedating medication and has no known allergies. Bedside glucose 5.2. T 38.7, HR 138, RR 28, SpO2 99 percent.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate plan?

new_options.text: Admit overnight for inpatient EEG and brain MRI
new_options.rationale: EEG and MRI are not indicated after a simple febrile seizure with normal neurology.

new_options.text: Lumbar puncture before discharge given age under 2 years
new_options.rationale: Routine LP for age under 2 is the anti-pattern: LP is not indicated after a simple febrile seizure in a well, immunised child without meningism who has returned to baseline.

new_options.text: Regular alternating paracetamol and ibuprofen to prevent recurrence
new_options.rationale: Aggressive antipyresis does not prevent recurrence of febrile seizures; antipyretics are for comfort only.

new_options.text: Reassurance, treat the febrile focus per eTG, discharge with written safety-net advice (recovery positioning, time the seizure, call ambulance if over 5 minutes), and routine GP follow-up; advise that recurrence risk is about 30 percent and risk of subsequent epilepsy is 1-2 percent (slightly above baseline)
new_options.rationale: This is a simple febrile seizure (single, generalised, under 15 minutes, full recovery, age 6 months to 6 years). Management is reassurance, treatment of the febrile focus, written first-aid advice and routine follow-up; no investigations are needed and no antiepileptic is indicated.

new_options.text: Prescribe maintenance sodium valproate at discharge
new_options.rationale: Antiepileptic prophylaxis is not used for simple febrile seizures.

verdicts.new_stem: A 9-month-old fully vaccinated boy is brought to his GP with 36 hours of low-grade fever (38.1 deg C) and mild irritability. He has had no rash, vomiting, diarrhoea or cough. He has 5-6 wet nappies a day and is feeding only slightly less than usual. He is otherwise well, on the 50th centile and developmentally normal. There were no antenatal renal concerns. He is uncircumcised. Examination: alert, well perfused, flat fontanelle, clear chest, soft non-tender abdomen, no palpable bladder, no flank tenderness. T 37.9, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 99 percent. The practice nurse applied an adhesive perineal bag for screening urinalysis. Bag urine: LE trace, nitrites negative, blood negative, protein negative, microscopy 8 WBC per high power field with no organisms seen. The lab offers same-day clean-catch processing.
verdicts.new_lead_in: How should the bag urine result be interpreted and what is the next step?

new_options.text: Bag urine has poor specificity but the negative result reliably excludes UTI; discharge with safety-net advice
new_options.rationale: Bag urine cannot reliably exclude UTI: nitrites require at least 4 hours of bladder dwell time (frequently negative in infants who void frequently), and not all uropathogens reduce nitrate.

new_options.text: Commence empirical oral trimethoprim while awaiting a repeat bag specimen
new_options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics should not be started on the basis of a bag urine alone; a reliable confirmatory sample is required first.

new_options.text: Negative nitrites rule out E. coli UTI in this child
new_options.rationale: Negative nitrites do not exclude UTI in infants because of short bladder dwell time and non-nitrate-reducing uropathogens.
source_refs: AAP - UTI in febrile infants 2-24 months

new_options.text: Send the bag specimen for culture; a negative culture will exclude UTI
new_options.rationale: Bag samples have contamination rates around 60 percent and do not meet Kass criteria; bag-urine culture cannot reliably exclude infection.

new_options.text: The bag result is unreliable for both diagnosing and excluding UTI; if clinical suspicion remains, obtain a CLEAN CATCH urine for microscopy and culture (catheter sample if the child is unwell or requires urgent treatment); do not start antibiotics on the basis of the bag urine
new_options.rationale: Bag-urine-as-diagnostic is the anti-pattern. Australian guidance (RCH CPG: UTI, NICE NG224): bag urine is for screening only because of high contamination and unreliable nitrites; a clean-catch (or catheter/SPA if unwell) is required for diagnosis. Antibiotics should not be started on bag urine alone.

verdicts.new_stem: A first-time mother attends with her 2-week-old ex-39-week breastfed son. The family has booked a religious circumcision next week. The infant is thriving, alert and well, with no jaundice, normal scrotum with bilateral palpable descended testes, and no inguinal hernia. The penis shows a glanular meatus opening on the ventral surface of the glans rather than the tip, a hooded incomplete dorsal foreskin, and mild ventral chordee on tractioned examination. There is no scrotal involvement, no bifid scrotum and no perineal abnormality. He passes urine in a normal forward stream. Antenatal scans showed no renal tract abnormality. Newborn examination at birth was documented as normal but appears to have missed these features. Paediatric urology offers outpatient review within 8 weeks.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate advice, and is karyotyping required?

new_options.text: Proceed with the planned circumcision because voiding is normal
new_options.rationale: Hypospadias contraindicates neonatal circumcision because the foreskin is required as tissue for urethroplasty (the circumcise-anyway anti-pattern).

new_options.text: Defer circumcision and refer to paediatric urology for assessment of distal hypospadias with mild chordee; the prepuce will be used for reconstruction (repair typically at 6-18 months); karyotyping/DSD workup is NOT required for isolated distal hypospadias with bilateral descended testes
new_options.rationale: Hypospadias is a contraindication to neonatal circumcision because the prepuce is needed for urethroplasty. Karyotype and DSD workup are reserved for proximal hypospadias, hypospadias plus undescended testis, or bifid scrotum; isolated distal hypospadias with bilateral descended testes does NOT require karyotyping.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Disorders of sex development

new_options.text: Proceed with partial circumcision preserving the dorsal hood for future repair
new_options.rationale: Even partial circumcision compromises the prepuce needed for urethroplasty; neonatal circumcision in hypospadias is avoided.

new_options.text: Reassure as a normal anatomical variant and arrange no follow-up
new_options.rationale: Distal hypospadias with chordee requires paediatric urology assessment and family counselling, even with normal voiding.

new_options.text: Arrange karyotype and pelvic ultrasound to exclude a disorder of sex development
new_options.rationale: DSD workup is reserved for proximal hypospadias, hypospadias with undescended testis or bifid scrotum, not isolated distal hypospadias with bilaterally descended testes.
source_refs: RCH CPG: Disorders of sex development

verdicts.new_stem: A primigravida attends a postnatal ward review at 8 hours after an uncomplicated term vaginal birth. The 32-week growth scan reported isolated SFU grade 2 left hydronephrosis with renal pelvis anteroposterior diameter 8 mm, normal contralateral kidney, normal ureters, normal bladder, normal amniotic fluid index and no other anomalies. The male infant (ex-40-week) is breastfeeding well, alert, well perfused with normal vitals. Examination: normal cardiorespiratory examination, no flank masses, bilateral descended testes, normal penis with glanular meatus, no spinal abnormality, normal hip examination, and a documented strong urinary stream. No family history of renal disease, VUR or consanguinity. The hospital offers outpatient renal ultrasound from day of life 4 onwards. Discharge home is planned for tomorrow.
verdicts.new_lead_in: When should the postnatal renal tract ultrasound be performed, and what additional imaging (if any) is needed at this stage?

new_options.text: Within the first 24 hours of life before discharge plus an MCUG
new_options.rationale: Imaging in the first 48 hours underestimates hydronephrosis because of physiological neonatal oliguria; routine MCUG is not indicated for isolated SFU 1-2.

new_options.text: Within the first 12 hours of life with concurrent MCUG
new_options.rationale: Early scanning and routine MCUG are both inappropriate for isolated mild-moderate antenatal hydronephrosis.

new_options.text: At 3 months of age routinely regardless of severity
new_options.rationale: Three-month deferral risks missing significant uropathy; SFU 2 should be assessed by 6 weeks.

new_options.text: Only if the infant develops a febrile illness or fails to thrive
new_options.rationale: Symptom-driven imaging misses asymptomatic obstructive uropathy and VUR; all antenatally detected hydronephrosis needs postnatal confirmation.

new_options.text: Renal tract ultrasound between day 4 and 6 weeks of life as an outpatient (typically days 4-7 or by 6 weeks); MCUG NOT required at this stage for isolated SFU 1-2; reserve MCUG for SFU 3-4, bilateral disease, abnormal postnatal ultrasound or suspected PUV, in which case earlier imaging (in the first days of life) is also indicated
new_options.rationale: RCH CPG: Antenatal hydronephrosis recommends postnatal renal ultrasound after day 4 once neonatal oliguria resolves; for isolated SFU 1-2 by 6 weeks is appropriate. SFU 3-4, bilateral disease, or suspected PUV warrant earlier imaging in the first days of life, and MCUG is reserved for higher-grade or abnormal findings.
source_refs: Society for Foetal Urology - Hydronephrosis grading

verdicts.new_stem: A 9-year-old previously well prepubertal boy (Tanner 1) presents to a regional ED with 24 hours of gradually worsening pain at the upper pole of the right testis. The pain is localised and dull rather than severe and has not been associated with nausea, vomiting or systemic upset. He has had no trauma, no urinary symptoms and no urethral discharge. His parents have noticed a small dark spot through the scrotal skin. He is alert and afebrile with normal vitals. The right hemiscrotum has mild diffuse swelling. The testis lies in normal vertical orientation with a normal cremasteric reflex. There is a discrete 5 mm point of tenderness at the UPPER POLE of the testis with a visible bluish discolouration through the thinned scrotal skin. The remainder of the testis is non-tender and palpable in normal position. The epididymis is non-tender. Urinalysis is negative.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most appropriate management is correct?

new_options.text: Acute testicular torsion; immediate scrotal exploration
new_options.rationale: Torsion typically has sudden severe pain, nausea, a high-riding horizontal testis and absent cremasteric reflex; this presentation does not fit.

new_options.text: Torsion of the appendix testis (torted hydatid); manage with analgesia and rest (NSAIDs and paracetamol), reassurance that the appendage infarcts and resolves spontaneously over 7-14 days, with safety-net advice to return if pain escalates or signs change; if diagnostic uncertainty remains, scrotal ultrasound with Doppler is the next step rather than empirical exploration
new_options.rationale: Subacute (12-48 hour) presentation in a prepubertal boy with localised upper-pole tenderness, visible blue dot, vertical lie and preserved cremasteric reflex is classic for torsion of the appendix testis. Australian management is conservative (analgesia, rest) because the appendage infarcts and the pain resolves; ultrasound with Doppler is reserved for diagnostic uncertainty.

new_options.text: Bacterial epididymo-orchitis; oral cefalexin and urology referral
new_options.rationale: Epididymo-orchitis features diffuse epididymal tenderness and positive urinalysis and is uncommon prepubertally without urinary tract abnormality.

new_options.text: Idiopathic scrotal oedema; observation alone
new_options.rationale: Idiopathic scrotal oedema presents with painless or mildly tender pink scrotal swelling that may extend to the perineum; there is no focal upper-pole tenderness or blue dot.

new_options.text: Incarcerated indirect inguinal hernia; immediate surgical reduction
new_options.rationale: Incarcerated hernia presents with a tender groin lump extending into the scrotum often with vomiting; examination here is intratesticular with no inguinal mass.

verdicts.new_stem: An 8-year-old boy presents to a regional ED with 3 weeks of polyuria, nocturia, polydipsia and 3 kg unintentional weight loss. He has been tired but is still attending school and eating normally. He has had no abdominal pain, no vomiting and no Kussmaul respiration. He has no past medical history, takes no medications, is fully immunised and has a maternal aunt with T1DM. He is alert and well perfused with no signs of dehydration. Fingerprick glucose 22 mmol/L, capillary blood ketones 1.8 mmol/L. VBG: pH 7.35, HCO3 19. Na 138, K 4.2. He has no acetone breath and no abdominal tenderness.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

new_options.text: Discharge home with urine dipstick and GP referral for fasting glucose and HbA1c tomorrow
new_options.rationale: Symptomatic hyperglycaemia with ketonaemia mandates same-day specialist paediatric review for insulin initiation and education, regardless of pH.

new_options.text: Commence IV 0.9% saline at twice maintenance and a fixed-rate insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour as per the DKA protocol
new_options.rationale: DKA-protocol fluid plus insulin is reserved for pH under 7.30 or HCO3 under 15 mmol/L. AU/ISPAD biochemical DKA criteria require pH under 7.30 or HCO3 under 15 with ketosis; this boy's pH 7.35 and HCO3 19 do not meet DKA threshold (he has hyperglycaemia with mild ketosis), so DKA-protocol management is incorrect.

new_options.text: Admit under the paediatric diabetes team for diagnosis confirmation (HbA1c, type 1 antibodies), subcutaneous insulin initiation (basal-bolus), and structured family education with dietetics
new_options.rationale: New-onset T1DM without DKA (pH 7.30 or more, HCO3 15 or more, ketones present but child clinically well) is admitted for subcutaneous insulin initiation, education and family training, per RCH CPG: Diabetes Mellitus and the APEG handbook.

new_options.text: Single oral dose of metformin 500 mg and paediatric endocrinology outpatient follow-up
new_options.rationale: Metformin is not first-line in suspected paediatric T1DM; insulin is required.

new_options.text: Observe in short stay for 4 hours and discharge if glucose falls below 15 mmol/L
new_options.rationale: Glucose will not self-correct without exogenous insulin in T1DM.

verdicts.new_stem: A 6-year-old girl with T1DM diagnosed 8 months ago is found by her father unresponsive in bed at 06:30. She is on basal-bolus insulin glargine 8 units at dinner and aspart with meals; she had a heavier than usual swimming session yesterday afternoon. Her father estimates her current weight at 22 kg. Capillary glucose 1.6 mmol/L. She is breathing normally with a strong pulse but does not respond to her name, withdraws only to a sternal rub, and is having intermittent generalised tonic-clonic jerking. There is no IV access at home. He has an in-date glucagon hypokit and an oral glucose gel in the fridge. Ambulance is 12 minutes away.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate action by her father?

new_options.text: Place oral glucose gel between her cheek and gum and roll her onto her side
new_options.rationale: Oral glucose is contraindicated in a child with reduced consciousness or active seizure because of aspiration risk; parenteral glucagon is indicated.

new_options.text: Administer IM glucagon 1 mg AND inject 2 units of insulin aspart to stabilise the response
new_options.rationale: 1 mg glucagon is the dose for children 25 kg or more; she is 22 kg. Insulin is contraindicated in active hypoglycaemia.

new_options.text: Wait for the ambulance and let paramedics administer glucagon in 12 minutes
new_options.rationale: Severe hypoglycaemia with seizure requires immediate treatment; delay risks hypoxic injury and prolonged seizure.

new_options.text: Administer IM glucagon 0.5 mg from the hypokit (because she weighs under 25 kg) and place her in the recovery position; recheck glucose after 10-15 minutes and call paramedics to confirm next steps
new_options.rationale: Severe hypoglycaemia (unconscious or seizing) in a child UNDER 25 kg is treated with IM glucagon 0.5 mg; 1 mg is for at least 25 kg. The AU-specific weight threshold is the discriminator. Recovery position and ambulance attendance complete the response.

new_options.text: Give buccal midazolam 0.3 mg/kg to terminate the seizure before glucose correction
new_options.rationale: Hypoglycaemic seizure terminates with glucose, not benzodiazepine; midazolam first risks airway compromise without correcting the cause.

verdicts.new_stem: A 12-day-old male term infant presents to a regional ED with 3 days of poor feeding, vomiting and increasing lethargy. He was born at 39+2 NVD, birth weight 3.4 kg, exclusively breastfed, discharged at 48 hours with a normal newborn examination. The heel-prick screen has not yet returned. He has lost 14 percent of birth weight. There is no diarrhoea or fever. He is pale, peripherally cool with CRT 4 seconds, sunken fontanelle and dry mucous membranes. Genitalia are normal male with bilaterally descended testes and no hyperpigmentation. On arrival: HR 188, RR 56, SpO2 96 percent on room air, T 36.2, BP 62/38. Bloods: Na 124, K 7.2, glucose 2.4, urea 11, creatinine 62, pH 7.22, HCO3 14.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which combination of most likely diagnosis and most discriminating biochemical signature is correct?

new_options.text: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis causing hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis
new_options.rationale: Pyloric stenosis gives hypochloraemic hypokalaemic ALKALOSIS; this infant has HYPERkalaemia and ACIDOSIS - the opposite electrolyte signature.

new_options.text: Late-onset group B streptococcal sepsis with septic shock and AKI
new_options.rationale: GBS can present with shock at this age but does not characteristically produce the specific Na low, K high, glucose low pattern; the electrolyte signature is the discriminator.

new_options.text: Urea cycle defect with hyperammonaemic encephalopathy
new_options.rationale: Urea cycle defects produce respiratory ALKALOSIS with hyperammonaemia, not hyperkalaemic metabolic acidosis.

new_options.text: Pseudohypoaldosteronism type 1 with systemic mineralocorticoid resistance
new_options.rationale: PHA1 shares the same biochemistry but is much rarer than 21-hydroxylase CAH; distinguished by elevated aldosterone with normal 17-OH progesterone, the opposite of CAH.

new_options.text: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency) with neonatal salt-wasting crisis; the discriminating biochemical signature is hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypoglycaemia + metabolic acidosis in a previously well day-7-14 neonate
new_options.rationale: Day 7-14 presentation with hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia, metabolic acidosis and shock in either sex is salt-wasting CAH from 21-hydroxylase deficiency; males have normal external genitalia and so are missed clinically until biochemical crisis, per RCH CPG: Adrenal Crisis and ESPE 2018.

verdicts.new_stem: A 7-year-old boy is referred to a paediatric developmental clinic for behavioural and learning difficulties. He was placed in long-term foster care at 3 months. His birth mother is documented to have consumed alcohol at hazardous levels throughout pregnancy. School reports document impulsivity, poor adaptive functioning, and specific maths and language deficits despite intensive support. Cognitive testing (educational psychologist) shows FSIQ 78 with significant scatter and severe impairment in three neurodevelopmental domains (executive function, adaptive behaviour, language). Physical growth tracks the 25th centile. On focused facial examination: smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border, and short palpebral fissures (below 3rd centile for age) - all three sentinel features present. There is no head injury, no seizure disorder and no other syndromic features. The foster carer is engaged with an Aboriginal community-controlled health service.
verdicts.new_lead_in: Which Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD category best applies?

new_options.text: Foetal Alcohol Syndrome without confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure
new_options.rationale: The exposure is confirmed; the without-confirmed-exposure category does not apply.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

new_options.text: ADHD combined presentation alone
new_options.rationale: ADHD may coexist but does not capture facial dysmorphology or the breadth of FASD-specific severe impairment.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

new_options.text: FASD with less than three sentinel facial features
new_options.rationale: He has all three sentinel features; this category does not apply.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

new_options.text: Static encephalopathy of unknown cause because measured IQ is above 70
new_options.rationale: FSIQ over 70 does not exclude FASD; the diagnosis is made on neurodevelopmental domain impairment, not global IQ alone.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD

new_options.text: FASD with three sentinel facial features: all three sentinel features (smooth philtrum, thin upper vermilion border, short palpebral fissures under 3rd centile), severe impairment in at least three of ten neurodevelopmental domains, and confirmed prenatal alcohol exposure
new_options.rationale: The Australian Guide diagnoses FASD with three sentinel facial features when all three are present together with severe impairment in three or more of ten neurodevelopmental domains and confirmed or assumed prenatal alcohol exposure - the specific AU diagnostic threshold tested here.
source_refs: Australian Guide to the Diagnosis of FASD
